questions.question: Explain the 'scaffolding' technique in chalk and talk teaching of Gram-Schmidt Process. How does breaking the orthogonalization into steps improve student comprehension?
keyPoints: Building from simple (one vector) to complex (full set)

questions.question: Design a 30-minute segment of an Inner Product Spaces lecture using the chalk and talk method. Specify what would be on the board, when you would pause for questions, and what annotations you would make.
Why is the geometric interpretation of the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality important in chalk and talk teaching? How would you present this on the board?
keyPoints: R^2/R^3 diagram showing angle between vectors

questions.question: Describe the 'progressive derivation' approach for teaching orthogonal projections. How does this help students transition from R^n to function spaces?
What are the key characteristics of effective annotation in the chalk and talk method when teaching Least Squares problems?
keyPoints: Examples of annotation for normal equations

questions.question: Design a complete 8-hour chalk and talk course on Inner Product Spaces. For each hour, specify: (i) specific topics to cover, (ii) key board layouts, (iii) anticipated difficult concepts and how to address them, (iv) student engagement techniques, and (v) closure activities.
keyPoints: Summary and preview for each hour
Integration of QR factorization and least squares

questions.question: Critically evaluate the chalk and talk method for teaching abstract linear algebra concepts like Inner Product Spaces. What are its strengths and limitations compared to technology-enhanced approaches? When would you recommend using alternative methods?
keyPoints: Strengths: real-time derivation, cognitive modeling, focus on fundamentals
Limitations: pace constraints, limited multi-media, physical demands
Comparison with interactive software, videos, online platforms
Specific topics where chalk and talk excels vs alternatives

questions.question: Explain how you would use the chalk and talk method to teach the connection between Gram-Schmidt Process, QR Factorization, and Least Squares. Design a teaching sequence that clearly shows these relationships.
keyPoints: Logical flow from orthogonalization to factorization to applications
Building QR from Gram-Schmidt steps on board
Geometric interpretation connecting projection to least squares
Normal equations as consequence of orthogonality
